name: Gambia
tagline: From the megalithic stone circles and the Mandinka kingdoms of the river, through the Atlantic slave trade and a narrow British enclave inside French Senegal, to independence in 1965, the Jammeh years, and a democratic transition in 2017.
summary: The Gambia is mainland Africa's smallest country, a sliver of territory wrapped around the lower 480 kilometres of the Gambia River and almost entirely enclosed by Senegal. Long before the modern state, the river valley held the Senegambian stone circles — over a thousand laterite megaliths and burial mounds raised across more than fifteen centuries — and fell within the reach of the medieval Mali Empire, whose Mandinka traders founded small riverine kingdoms such as Niumi. From the mid-fifteenth century the river drew Portuguese, then British and French traders, and became a notorious artery of the Atlantic slave trade centred on James Island, now Kunta Kinteh Island, a UNESCO World Heritage Site near the village of Juffure. Britain shaped its possession into a narrow enclave following the river through a series of boundary agreements with France, governing the colony of Bathurst (now Banjul) and an inland protectorate. The Gambia became independent of the United Kingdom on 18 February 1965 under Dawda Jawara, a republic in 1970, briefly joined Senegal in the Senegambia Confederation (1982–1989), endured the 22-year authoritarian rule of Yahya Jammeh after his 1994 coup, and saw a peaceful transfer of power to Adama Barrow following the December 2016 election, secured with ECOWAS backing in January 2017.

founding.label: Independence from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: On 18 February 1965 The Gambia became an independent state within the Commonwealth, ending British rule over a colony and protectorate that Britain had assembled along the Gambia River over the previous century and a half. The new country was a constitutional monarchy with Elizabeth II as head of state, led by Prime Minister Dawda Kairaba Jawara; the United States acknowledged Gambian independence the same day, when Assistant Secretary of State G. Mennen Williams attended the ceremonies in the capital, then named Bathurst. The Gambia was unusual among newly independent African states: a narrow enclave only some tens of kilometres wide, stretching inland along the river and surrounded on three sides by French-speaking Senegal, a shape inherited directly from nineteenth-century Anglo-French boundary agreements. Independence was the culmination of a far longer story — the megalithic stone circles of the river valley, the reach of the Mali Empire and the Mandinka kingdoms, and centuries in which the Gambia River served as one of West Africa's great trading waterways and a conduit of the Atlantic slave trade. The Gambia became a republic on 24 April 1970, with Jawara as its first president.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: From the United Kingdom on 18 February 1965; a republic from 24 April 1970

quickFacts.label: First leader
quickFacts.value: Sir Dawda Kairaba Jawara — prime minister from 1962, then first president (1970–1994)

quickFacts.label: Geography
quickFacts.value: Mainland Africa's smallest country: a narrow enclave along the Gambia River, almost surrounded by Senegal

quickFacts.label: World Heritage
quickFacts.value: Stone Circles of Senegambia (2006) and Kunta Kinteh Island and Related Sites (2003)

quickFacts.label: Colonial capital
quickFacts.value: Bathurst, founded 1816 on St Mary's Island; renamed Banjul in 1973

quickFacts.label: Democratic transition
quickFacts.value: Adama Barrow defeated Yahya Jammeh in December 2016; power transferred in January 2017 with ECOWAS support

eras.title: Stone circles and the peoples of the river
eras.standfirst: Along the Gambia River, a prosperous and organised society raised more than a thousand laterite megaliths and burial mounds over fifteen centuries, long before the Mandinka, Wolof, Fula and Serer who people the land today.
body: The lower and middle reaches of the Gambia River hold one of the great concentrations of megalithic monuments in Africa: the Senegambian stone circles. Spread in a band roughly 100 kilometres wide along some 350 kilometres of the river and its hinterland, straddling the modern Gambia–Senegal frontier, they comprise over a thousand stone circles of cut laterite pillars and their associated burial mounds, or tumuli. The pillars were quarried with iron tools and stand on average about two metres high; the tallest, at Wassu in The Gambia, reaches 2.59 metres. Excavated burials have yielded dates broadly between the third century BCE and the sixteenth century CE, with mounds near Wassu dated to roughly the tenth to fourteenth centuries CE.

Together the circles and mounds form a vast sacred landscape, built and reused over more than 1,500 years, that archaeologists read as the work of a settled, prosperous and highly organised society. Scholars have not reached consensus on exactly which people raised them, but the monuments clearly predate the present Mandinka inhabitants of the region. Four representative groups — Wassu and Kerr Batch (Kerbatch) in The Gambia, and Sine Ngayène and Wanar in Senegal — were inscribed together on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2006 as the Stone Circles of Senegambia, one of only a few trans-boundary World Heritage properties in Africa.

By the time the circles fell out of use, the Senegambian region was home to the ancestors of its principal modern peoples: the Mandinka (Malinke), the Wolof, the Fula (Fulani) and the Serer, among others such as the Jola. Their settlement of the river valley and the surrounding savanna unfolded over centuries through migration and the spread of farming, herding and trade, and it set the human stage on which the medieval West African empires and the river kingdoms would later rise.

events.title: Megalith-building begins along the river
events.summary: Communities in the Senegambian region began raising circles of cut laterite pillars and burial mounds along the Gambia River; excavated burials yield dates spanning roughly the third century BCE to the sixteenth century CE.

events.title: A sacred landscape of more than a thousand monuments
events.summary: Over fifteen centuries a prosperous, organised society created more than 1,000 stone circles and tumuli across a band some 100 km wide along 350 km of the river; burials near Wassu date to roughly AD 900–1300.

events.title: Settlement of the Mandinka, Wolof, Fula and Serer
events.summary: The ancestors of the region's principal peoples — Mandinka, Wolof, Fula and Serer — settled the river valley and savanna through migration and the spread of farming, herding and trade, predating the river kingdoms.

pullquote.text: Over more than fifteen centuries, the people of the river raised a thousand stone circles — a sacred landscape that long predates today's inhabitants.
pullquote.attribution: Stone Circles of Senegambia, UNESCO World Heritage

eras.title: The Mali Empire and the Mandinka kingdoms
eras.standfirst: In the thirteenth century the reach of the Mali Empire extended to the Gambia, and Mandinka traders and warriors founded small kingdoms — Niumi, Niani, Wuli — that controlled the river and its commerce.
body: From the thirteenth century the Gambia River valley fell within the western sphere of the Mali Empire, the great Mande state that rose under Sundiata Keita and at its height dominated much of West Africa, carrying Islam, long-distance trade and a sophisticated administration across the savanna. Mandinka (Malinke) traders and warriors, expanding from the empire's heartland on the upper Niger, settled the banks of the Gambia and established a patchwork of small kingdoms that paid tribute to, or drew their legitimacy from, the wider Mande world.

Several of these riverine states endured for centuries. Niumi, also called Barra, lay at the mouth of the river on its north bank, extending some 60 kilometres along and inland from the coast; its Mandinka ruling clans grew wealthy by taxing traffic on the river and levying tolls on the European ships that later came to trade. Upstream lay other Mandinka kingdoms such as Niani and Wuli, the latter controlling routes that linked the upper Niger valley to the coast. Alongside the Mandinka, the Wolof, Fula and Serer formed their own polities and communities across Senegambia, in a mosaic of states bound together by trade, kinship and a common riverine economy.

The Gambia River itself was the organising feature of this world — navigable far inland, it served as a highway for salt, kola, ivory, gold, cloth and captives moving between the interior and the coast. Control of crossing points, landing places and stretches of bank translated directly into power and revenue, a logic that would shape the kingdoms' dealings with the European traders who began to arrive in the mid-fifteenth century.

events.title: Mali's reach extends to the Gambia
events.summary: The Mali Empire expanded into the Gambia River valley in the thirteenth century, bringing the region within the Mande world and spreading Islam, trade and administration as Mandinka traders moved west.

events.title: Mandinka kingdoms of the river
events.summary: Mandinka warriors and traders founded small states along both banks of the Gambia, including Niumi (Barra), Niani and Wuli, which controlled river trade and important inland routes.

events.title: Niumi commands the river mouth
events.summary: The Mandinka kingdom of Niumi, controlled in rotation by clans such as the Jamme, Maane and Sonko, grew prosperous by taxing merchant traffic at the mouth of the Gambia River and would later levy tolls on European vessels.

pullquote.text: Navigable far inland, the Gambia was a highway for salt, gold, ivory and captives — and control of its banks meant power.

eras.title: The river, European trade and the Atlantic slave trade
eras.standfirst: From 1455 the Gambia drew Portuguese, then British and French traders, and the river became a notorious artery of the Atlantic slave trade — centred on James Island, now Kunta Kinteh Island, near the village of Juffure.
body: In 1455–1456 ships sailing for Portugal's Prince Henry the Navigator, under the Venetian Alvise Cadamosto and the Genoese Antoniotto Usodimare, entered the Gambia River — the first recorded European voyage there. The Portuguese established trading contacts and small settlements such as San Domingo, and gave the midstream island near the river mouth its first European name. The river's deep, navigable channel made it exceptionally valuable to Europeans, allowing seagoing ships to penetrate far inland to trade for gold, ivory, beeswax, hides and, increasingly, enslaved people.

From the mid-seventeenth century the island at the centre of the river changed hands repeatedly. Settled briefly by the Duchy of Courland, it was taken by the English in 1661, who renamed it James Island and built Fort James, named for James, Duke of York. Chartered English companies — the Royal Adventurers and then the Royal African Company — ran the fort, and the trade shifted decisively toward human beings. France and England struggled over the river for more than a century; the fort was captured several times, and between 1765 and 1783 the Gambia formed part of the British colony of Senegambia. James Island, with the nearby villages of Juffure and Albreda, became one of the most important slaving stations on the river, a place where captives from the interior were held before being shipped across the Atlantic.

This was a catastrophe of immense and lasting human cost. James Island and its related sites — including Juffure, Albreda and the ruined Portuguese chapel and warehouses — were inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2003 as a memorial to the slave trade and its eventual abolition; the island was renamed Kunta Kinteh Island in 2011. The name recalls the central figure of Alex Haley's 1976 book Roots, in which Haley traced his ancestry to a man he called Kunta Kinte, said to have been taken from the Juffure area and carried to Maryland in the eighteenth century. Haley's account is a work of family memory and historical fiction whose specific genealogical claims scholars have questioned; the island and Juffure remain, factually, an enduring site of memory of the Atlantic trade. Britain abolished its own slave trade in 1807 and afterward built fortifications such as Fort Bullen at the river mouth to help suppress the traffic it had once carried.

events.title: Portuguese ships enter the Gambia
events.summary: Expeditions under Cadamosto and Usodimare, sailing for Portugal's Prince Henry the Navigator, reached the Gambia River and opened European trade, establishing the first contacts and small settlements along its banks.

events.title: England takes James Island
events.summary: English forces seized the midstream island, renaming it James Island and building Fort James; chartered companies ran the post, and its trade shifted from gold and ivory toward enslaved Africans.

events.title: The Atlantic slave trade on the river
events.summary: James Island, with the villages of Juffure and Albreda, became a major slaving station; captives from the interior were held there before being shipped across the Atlantic, while England and France fought repeatedly for control of the river.

events.title: Britain abolishes its slave trade
events.summary: Britain outlawed its transatlantic slave trade and later built defences such as Fort Bullen at the river mouth to help suppress slaving — among the few regional structures built against the trade rather than for it.

events.title: Kunta Kinteh Island recognised
events.summary: James Island and Related Sites were inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2003 as a memorial to the slave trade; the island was renamed Kunta Kinteh Island in 2011, recalling the figure in Alex Haley's Roots associated with nearby Juffure.

pullquote.text: James Island and the village of Juffure stand at the mouth of the Gambia as an enduring memory of the Atlantic slave trade.
pullquote.attribution: Kunta Kinteh Island and Related Sites, UNESCO World Heritage

eras.title: The British colony and the river enclave
eras.standfirst: Britain built its possession around the colony of Bathurst and an inland protectorate, fixing through Anglo-French agreements the borders of a narrow enclave that followed the Gambia River within French-surrounded Senegal.
body: British involvement on the river hardened in the nineteenth century. In 1816 Captain Alexander Grant founded Bathurst (modern Banjul) on St Mary's Island near the river mouth, after an agreement with the local ruler of Kombo; the settlement became Britain's administrative base for suppressing the slave trade and for commerce in the new staple, groundnuts (peanuts). Over the following decades Britain extended its authority up the river through treaties and force, while the older Mandinka kingdoms such as Niumi were drawn into its orbit and finally absorbed.

The Gambia's distinctive shape was the product of imperial bargaining between Britain and France, which was meanwhile building the vast colony of Senegal all around it. A series of Anglo-French boundary agreements in the 1880s and 1890s fixed the frontier as a narrow band following the river, leaving The Gambia almost entirely enclosed by French territory — a colonial accident that has defined the country ever since. By 1902 Britain governed the territory as two units: the small Colony of the Gambia, centred on Bathurst, and the much larger Protectorate of the inland districts, ruled indirectly through local chiefs.

For most of the colonial period The Gambia was a quiet, lightly developed possession whose economy rested overwhelmingly on groundnut exports. Constitutional change came slowly after the Second World War. A new constitution in 1960 created a House of Representatives, and following elections Dawda Jawara — a British-trained veterinary surgeon who led the Protectorate-based People's Progressive Party — became the dominant political figure, serving as premier from 1962 and steering the colony through internal self-government toward full independence.

events.title: Bathurst founded on St Mary's Island
events.summary: Captain Alexander Grant established the settlement of Bathurst (now Banjul) near the mouth of the Gambia, creating Britain's base for suppressing the slave trade and trading in groundnuts.

events.title: Anglo-French agreements draw the enclave
events.summary: Boundary agreements between Britain and France fixed The Gambia's frontiers as a narrow band following the river, leaving the territory almost entirely surrounded by French Senegal.

events.title: Colony and Protectorate established
events.summary: Britain organised its possession into the Colony of the Gambia, centred on Bathurst, and a larger inland Protectorate governed indirectly through local chiefs, completing the colonial framework.

events.title: Jawara leads toward self-rule
events.summary: After a new 1960 constitution created a House of Representatives, Dawda Jawara of the People's Progressive Party became premier in 1962, leading the colony through internal self-government toward independence.

pullquote.text: A series of Anglo-French agreements fixed the border as a thin band along the river, leaving the colony almost entirely enclosed by French Senegal — a shape it keeps to this day.

eras.title: Independence, republic and the Jawara years
eras.standfirst: The Gambia became independent of the United Kingdom on 18 February 1965 under Dawda Jawara, a republic in 1970, and — after a 1981 coup attempt put down with Senegalese help — joined Senegal in the short-lived Senegambia Confederation.
body: On 18 February 1965 The Gambia became independent within the Commonwealth, at first as a constitutional monarchy with Elizabeth II as queen and Dawda Jawara as prime minister. Many observers doubted that so small and narrow a country, dependent on a single crop and almost surrounded by Senegal, could survive as a separate state, but it did. After an earlier referendum failed to reach the required majority, a second succeeded, and on 24 April 1970 The Gambia became a republic, with Jawara as its first president.

Sir Dawda Kairaba Jawara went on to lead the country for nearly three decades, and his rule was, by the standards of the era, notably moderate: he maintained a multi-party system and avoided the worst authoritarian excesses seen elsewhere in the region, even as his People's Progressive Party dominated successive elections. In 1981, while Jawara was abroad, a violent coup attempt led by Kukoi Samba Sanyang briefly seized Bathurst; Jawara invoked a mutual-defence agreement and Senegalese troops intervened to crush the revolt and restore him, at considerable loss of life.

The 1981 crisis drew The Gambia and Senegal closer. On 1 February 1982, under a treaty signed the previous December, the two countries formed the Senegambia Confederation, intended to integrate their armed forces, security, economies and currencies while each retained its sovereignty; Senegal's President Abdou Diouf served as confederation president and Jawara as vice-president. The union never won broad popular enthusiasm, and disputes over economic integration — particularly Senegal's wish to end The Gambia's lucrative re-export trade — undermined it. After Senegal withdrew its troops in 1989, the confederation was formally dissolved on 30 September 1989, and the two states resumed fully separate paths.

events.title: Independence from the United Kingdom
events.summary: The Gambia became independent within the Commonwealth as a constitutional monarchy, with Dawda Jawara as prime minister; the United States acknowledged independence the same day at ceremonies in Bathurst.

events.title: The Gambia becomes a republic
events.summary: After a second referendum, The Gambia adopted a republican constitution and Dawda Jawara became its first president, severing the last formal ties of British sovereignty.

events.title: Coup attempt and Senegalese intervention
events.summary: A violent attempt to seize power led by Kukoi Samba Sanyang briefly took Bathurst while Jawara was abroad; Senegalese troops intervened under a defence pact to crush the revolt and restore him.

events.title: The Senegambia Confederation formed
events.summary: The Gambia and Senegal established the Senegambia Confederation to integrate their militaries, security and economies while retaining sovereignty, with Senegal's Abdou Diouf as president and Jawara as vice-president.

events.title: The confederation dissolves
events.summary: Lacking popular support and strained by disputes over economic and customs integration, the Senegambia Confederation was formally dissolved after Senegal withdrew its troops, and the two states resumed separate paths.

figures.name: Sir Dawda Kairaba Jawara
figures.role: First Prime Minister and President of The Gambia
figures.blurb: British-trained veterinary surgeon and leader of the People's Progressive Party who led The Gambia to independence in 1965, became its first president in 1970, and governed for nearly three decades as a relatively moderate, multi-party ruler until he was overthrown in 1994.

pullquote.text: Few believed so small and narrow a country, almost surrounded by Senegal, could stand alone — yet it did.

eras.title: The Jammeh era and the 2017 transition
eras.standfirst: Yahya Jammeh seized power in a 1994 coup and ruled for 22 increasingly authoritarian years, until his defeat by Adama Barrow in December 2016 and a peaceful transfer of power in January 2017, secured with ECOWAS backing.
body: On 22 July 1994 a group of young army officers led by Lieutenant Yahya Jammeh overthrew Dawda Jawara in a bloodless coup, ending the only government independent Gambia had known. Jammeh, who chaired a military council before contriving a transition to civilian rule, was declared the winner of a presidential election in 1996 and was sworn in as president that November. He went on to win further contests in 2001, 2006 and 2011 and held power for 22 years.

Jammeh's long rule grew steadily more authoritarian and repressive. His government suppressed the press, detained and tortured opponents, and was implicated in enforced disappearances and extrajudicial killings; he made notorious threats against perceived enemies and minorities and, in 2009, presided over a widely condemned witch-hunting campaign. A Truth, Reconciliation and Reparations Commission later documented a pattern of grave human-rights abuses across his presidency. In 2013 Jammeh withdrew The Gambia from the Commonwealth and in 2015 declared it an Islamic republic.

The presidential election of 1 December 2016 produced an upset: Adama Barrow, a real-estate businessman backed by an opposition coalition, defeated Jammeh. Jammeh at first conceded but reversed himself a week later and rejected the result, triggering a constitutional crisis. Barrow was inaugurated at the Gambian embassy in Dakar on 19 January 2017, and the regional bloc ECOWAS, with United Nations authorisation, deployed troops to enforce the outcome. Under that pressure Jammeh agreed to step down and went into exile in Equatorial Guinea on 21 January 2017; Barrow returned to take office at home days later. The Gambia subsequently rejoined the Commonwealth in 2018 and embarked on a process of democratic and institutional reform, with Barrow re-elected in 2021.

events.title: Jammeh's coup ends the Jawara era
events.summary: Lieutenant Yahya Jammeh and fellow officers overthrew President Dawda Jawara in a bloodless coup, installing a military council and ending the only government independent Gambia had known.

events.title: Jammeh elected president
events.summary: After a transition to civilian rule under a new constitution, Jammeh was declared winner of the 1996 presidential election and sworn in, beginning a tenure he would extend through further votes in 2001, 2006 and 2011.

events.title: Repression and abuses under Jammeh
events.summary: Jammeh's rule was marked by suppression of the press, detentions, torture, enforced disappearances and a 2009 witch-hunting campaign; a later Truth, Reconciliation and Reparations Commission documented widespread abuses.

events.title: Barrow defeats Jammeh
events.summary: Adama Barrow, backed by an opposition coalition, won the presidential election; Jammeh initially conceded but a week later rejected the result, precipitating a constitutional crisis.

events.title: ECOWAS intervention and transfer of power
events.summary: Barrow was sworn in at the Gambian embassy in Dakar on 19 January 2017; with ECOWAS troops deployed under UN authorisation, Jammeh agreed to leave and went into exile in Equatorial Guinea on 21 January, completing a peaceful handover.

figures.name: Yahya Jammeh
figures.blurb: Army officer who seized power in the 1994 coup and ruled for 22 years through increasingly repressive means, documented later by a truth commission, before losing the 2016 election and going into exile in Equatorial Guinea in January 2017.

figures.name: Adama Barrow
figures.blurb: Real-estate businessman and opposition-coalition candidate who defeated Yahya Jammeh in December 2016 and took office in January 2017 after ECOWAS intervention, leading The Gambia's democratic transition and winning re-election in 2021.

pullquote.text: After 22 years and a week-long refusal to concede, Jammeh left for exile under ECOWAS pressure — and power changed hands.

figures.name: Sir Dawda Kairaba Jawara
figures.blurb: Led The Gambia to independence from Britain on 18 February 1965, became its first president when it turned republic in 1970, and governed for nearly three decades as a relatively moderate, multi-party ruler until the 1994 coup.

figures.name: Yahya Jammeh
figures.blurb: Seized power in the 1994 coup and ruled for 22 increasingly authoritarian years before his defeat by Adama Barrow and departure into exile in January 2017.

figures.name: Adama Barrow
figures.blurb: Opposition-coalition candidate whose December 2016 victory over Jammeh, secured by an ECOWAS intervention in January 2017, ended 22 years of authoritarian rule and began a democratic transition.

sources.label: Kunta Kinteh Island and Related Sites
A Guide to the History of Recognition: The Gambia
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian
